coredata.dc:description: Photo-Fenton process is acknowledged for its high efficiency and technical feasibility in water treatment; however, its application is constrained by the addition of iron ions and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Herein, an efficient photo-self-Fenton system with coating Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) on the surface of highly crystalline C3N5 (HC-C3N5) nanosheets was developed for degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. Experimental results reveal that Fe-CDs not only serve as active sites for facilitating superior light-induced charge separation and transfer towards the in-situ generation of H2O2 over HC-C3N5, but also provide the solid-phase Fe species for maximizing the activation of in-situ generated H2O2 to produce highly oxidative OH and O2 – species towards the photo-self-Fenton reaction. This research presents a novel design approach of utilizing a Fe-CDs-based photo-self-Fenton system for efficient wastewater treatment.

head.ce:title: Boosted photo-self-Fenton degradation activity by Fe-doped carbon dots as dual-function active sites for in-situ H2O2 generation and activation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo-Fenton process is acknowledged for its high efficiency and technical feasibility in water treatment; however, its application is constrained by the addition of iron ions and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Herein, an efficient photo-self-Fenton system with coating Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) on the surface of highly crystalline C3N5 (HC-C3N5) nanosheets was developed for degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. Experimental results reveal that Fe-CDs not only serve as active sites for facilitating superior light-induced charge separation and transfer towards the in-situ generation of H2O2 over HC-C3N5, but also provide the solid-phase Fe species for maximizing the activation of in-situ generated H2O2 to produce highly oxidative OH and O2 – species towards the photo-self-Fenton reaction. This research presents a novel design approach of utilizing a Fe-CDs-based photo-self-Fenton system for efficient wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic residues pose a long-term pollution threat to the natural environment, particularly to aquatic systems and soil, potentially increasing the toxicity to aquatic organisms and spreading microbial resistance, thereby destabilizing entire ecosystems [1–4]. Consequently, the treatment of antibiotic wastewater has become a critical issue in ensuring sustainable human survival in contemporary society [5,6]. In the traditional treatment of such wastewater technology, the Fenton oxidation method has the advantages of simple, high efficiency and rapid reaction, Fe2+ can catalyze the activation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to produce strong oxidized reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can rapidly degrade the vast majority of organic pollutants into small, nontoxic and harmless molecules [7,8]. However, the process still has the following limitations: (i) during the Fenton reaction, the addition of H2O2 reagent leads in high treatment costs [9]; (ii) narrow pH application range (pH ≤ 3) [10]. Recently, the photo-self-Fenton system has attracted extensive attention due to its ability to generate H2O2 in-situ by the photocatalyst and combine with Fe2+ ions to continuously produce high-active hydroxyl (OH) radicals for the efficient degradation of organic contaminants [11]. For example, Wu et al. developed a photocatalytic self-Fenton degradation system based on the carbon-deficient g-C3N4, as the H2O2 generator, combined with additional Fe2+ ions to obtain a surpassing degradation rate constant of 0.07 min−1 for the degradation of 2,4-Dichlorophenol [12]. What’s more besides, Zhang et al. designed a photo-self-Fenton system with using the non-separated low-cost resorcinol formaldehyde resin for in-situ production of abundant H2O2, realizing the rapid cycling efficiency of excess iron ions in Fenton-catalyzed degradation of bisphenol A with achieving a high mineralization efficiency (67 %) by directly opening aromatic rings [13]. Although the above research works have demonstrated the potential of photo-self-Fenton system, Fe2+ ions still added to this system, which can lead to significant deposition of iron-based waste as well as slow reaction kinetics [14]. Consequently, exploring more efficient photo-self-Fenton techniques without additional Fe2+ has become the research frontier of advanced photocatalytic technologies.

Existing studies have shown that the introduction of iron-based solid materials can effectively activate in-situ generated H2O2 by the photocatalyst substrate, forming an efficient heterogeneous photo-self-Fenton system for water treatment [15]. For instance, Sun et al. have masterfully engineered a photo-self-Fenton degradation system of combining cadmium sulfide (CdS) with industrial waste red mud (RM), which can not only effectively activate the H₂O₂ produced in the system, but also can effectively form a S-scheme heterojunction with the CdS by the built-in electric field (IEF) at the interface and accelerate the charge separation [16]. In addition, Guo's group has successfully engineered a photo-self-Fenton system with decorating the Bi2Fe4O9 nanosheets on the ZnIn2S4 micron flowers to construct a 2D/2D van der Waals heterojunction, which creates an enlarged interfacial area providing abundant active sites, thereby efficiently utilizing the in-situ generated H2O2.Despite the remarkable H₂O₂ activation ability of the above iron-based materials, their voluminous nature predominantly exhibits bulk characteristics, thus exposing only a scant number of reactive sites for H₂O₂ activation, which constricts the overall activation efficacy of the iron ions [17]. Additionally, preceding research underscores that in photo-self-Fenton reactions, the majority of iron materials are relegated to merely serving as iron sources [18], which did not exhibit a promoting effect in the H₂O₂ in-situ production of photocatalyst substrates. Therefore, the development of effective small-size iron-containing species materials that can synchronously promote the generation and activation of H₂O₂ in-situ is of great significance for the photo-self-Fenton system.

Carbon dots (CDs), with dimensions less than 10 nm, have emerged as a highly appealing class of carbon nanomaterials. Their allure stems from a wealth of surface functional groups, exceptional electron storage capacities, photoinduced electron transfer characteristics, and highly efficient and stable active centers, attributing collectively endow CDs with significant potential for diverse applications across various scientific and technological domains [19]. For the field of photocatalysis, especially in the photocatalytic H₂O₂ production, CDs can be used as nonmetallic co-catalysts to modify semiconductors for obtaining excellent performance. For instance, Wang et al. developed a CDs-based metal-free photocatalyst, which facilitate charge separation and transfer, efficiently convert oxygen into H2O2 via a dual-electron pathway by capturing electrons and regulating the interface charge transport kinetics [20]. Furthermore, Li et al. established a self-floating CDs/conjugated microporous polymer (CDs/CMP) photocatalytic system, in which CDs act as “bridges” for electron transfer, modulating the surface hydrophobicity of CMP, thereby enhancing charge transfer, optimizing the reaction interface, and achieving highly efficient H2O2 production with the rate of 8542.6 μmol g-1h−1 [21]. Inspired by the above analysis, integrating iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) as dual active sites with a suitable photocatalyst substrate capable of producing H2O2 to form a photo-self-Fenton system may provide an effective catalyst design scheme for the treatment of antibiotic wastewater.

In this work, we engineered a photo-self-Fenton system by integrating highly crystalline C3N5 (HC-C3N5) nanosheets, which serve as photocatalysts for H2O2 production, with Fe-CDs that act as dual-function active sites to enhance both the in-situ generation and activation of H2O2. This system was utilized for the degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. Through a comprehensive suite of experimental techniques and characterization methods, we conducted an in-depth analysis of the TC degradation mechanism within the photo-self-Fenton system. Furthermore, the toxicity of the TC degradation intermediates was assessed using ECOSAR, highlighting potential risks associated with the photo-self-Fenton treatment process. These findings offer crucial insights and foundational support for advancing sustainable and efficient photocatalytic environmental remediation technologies

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe-doped CDs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fe-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CDs) were synthesized using a straightforward hydrothermal method according to previous report [21]. Initially, 0.24 mmol of iron (II) acetate and 1.67 mmol of 1,10-phenanthroline were dissolved in 15 ml of ethanol and heated at 60 °C for 4 h to form a homogeneous solution. This mixture was subsequently transferred into a 30 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 160 °C for 24 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the mixture was centrifuged to obtain a brown colloidal solution of Fe-CDs. For comparison, carbon quantum dots (CDs) without iron doping were synthesized using the same procedure, but without the addition of iron (II) acetate.

High-crystal C3N5 nanosheets (HC-C3N5) were prepared by alkali-potassium salt-assisted thermal polymerization. In this process, 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole, KCl and KOH with a mass ratio of 1:2:0.1 are ground in agate mortar. Subsequently, the resulting mixture underwent a thermal treatment in a muffle furnace, where the temperature was gradually increased to 600 °C at a rate of 2 °C/min and maintained for 4 h. Upon completion of the heating process, the product was allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature, then washed by boiling deionized water to eliminate any residual metal salts. Finally, the bright yellow HC-C3N5 product obtained was dried thoroughly and stored for subsequent application. For the comparison, the bulk C3N5 was prepared using the one-step calcination method based on the previous report [22]. In general, 3-amino-1,2, 4-triazole is heated in a Muffle furnace to 550 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, wash with deionized water and ethanol several times to remove the remaining precursors.

Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composites were synthesized via a hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Initially, a predetermined number of Fe-CDs and 100 mg of HC-C3N5 were dissolved and uniformly dispersed in 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol through vigorous stirring for 30 min. This homogenous mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed, and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 12 h. Post reaction, the autoclave was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. The resultant precipitate was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and subsequently washed multiple times with distilled water to eliminate any residual reactants and solvents. The dried samples were then collected and stored in a desiccator for further use. Through this procedure, x% Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composites were successfully synthesized, where x denotes the weight percentage (wt.%) of Fe-CDs relative to HC-C3N5, specifically x = 1, 2, 3, and 4. The complete synthesis process is illustrated in Scheme 1. For comparative purposes, a 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5 composite was also prepared following the same procedure as for 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, with the substitution of Fe-CDs by CDs.

A range of material characterizations, photocatalytic performance tests and standard curves for H2O2 production (Fig.S1) were listed in the Supporting Information
.

The structures and morphologies of B-C3N5, HC-C3N5, and Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 have been extensively studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) techniques. Distinct two-dimensional nano-sheet structures were revealed in Fig. 1 a and c, indicative of the layered characteristics inherent to both B-C3N5 and HC-C3N5. The absence of clear lattice fringes in B-C3N5 was demonstrated by HRTEM imaging in Fig. 1b, implying its amorphous nature. Notably, when HC-C3N5 nanosheets underwent eutectic salt (KCl/KOH) annealing, prominent lattice fringes were exhibited, suggestive of enhanced crystallinity [23]. Fig. 1d reveals two distinct lattice spacings, quantified at 1.02 and 0.32 nm. The lattice spacing of 1.02 nm corresponds to the extension length of repeating units along the (100) diffraction plane. In contrast, the 0.32 nm spacing is indicative of the stacking interactions of π-π conjugated aromatic rings within the (002) diffraction plane [24]. Simultaneously, according to the TEM images (Fig.S2), the Fe-CDs have an average diameter of approximately 3–4 nm and exhibit good dispersibility. Fig. 1e and f vividly depict the dense anchoring of Fe-CDs on the surface of HC-C3N5, resulting in the formation of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composites. Notably, no lattice fringes associated with Fe-CDs, which may be due to the formation of Fe-N substances that cause Fe-CDs to exist in an amorphous state [21]. Meanwhile, the original highly crystalline nano-sheet morphology of HC-C3N5, along with the corresponding lattice spacings for HC-C3N5, was retained by the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 catalyst, as demonstrated in Fig. 1g, intimating coupling between the amorphous and highly crystalline interfaces enhances the rapid transfer of photo-generated electrons across the interface while concurrently providing active sites for the in-situ generation of H2O2 and the subsequent Fenton reaction. Moreover, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) spectra (Fig.S3) and high-angle annular dark-field scanning electron microscopy (HAADF-SEM) elemental mapping images (Fig. 2 h) confirmed the homogeneous distribution of Fe, C, and N elements within the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite material. These results validate the successful synthesis of the composite material.

Employing X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, the elucidation of the crystalline structure of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 was advanced. Fig.S4 and Fig. 2a delineates the XRD patterns of Fe-CDs, B-C3N5, HC-C3N5 and diverse mass ratios of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5. The Fe-CDs exhibit a sharp peak around 21°, corresponding to the (120) crystalline plane of carbon. In addition, B-C3N5, HC-C3N5 and diverse mass ratios of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 all manifests two distinct peaks corresponding to the intra-layer and inter-layer stacking of g-C3N5. Upon meticulous scrutiny of the XRD local graph (Fig. 2b), a discernible shift is noted in the (100) peak of HC-C3N5 towards a lower angle (7.9°) relative to B-C3N5 (13.2°), ascribed to the incursion of K into the heptazine units of HC-C3N5 [25]. Furthermore, the characteristic peak (002) of HC-C3N5 experiences an augmentation towards a higher angle (28.3°) in contrast to B-C3N5 (27.5°), signifying a denser interlayer stacking facilitated by fortified π-π interactions among adjacent heptazine layers [26]. Moreover, the (002) diffraction peak of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 progressively shifts towards lower angles with escalating Fe-CDs content, indicative of a certain degree of Fe-CDs intercalation, thus augmenting the interlayer spacing of C3N5 nanosheets. This intercalation fosters the genesis of heterogeneous or homogeneous interfaces, amplifying photonic efficiency and orchestrating active sites at the interface to catalyze photocatalytic reactions [21]. Additionally, XRD findings underscore the conspicuous absence of characteristic peaks attributed to iron or iron oxides across all samples, implying the highly dispersed nature of Fe species on the surface of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, plausibly existing in the form of Fe-N [19]. To gain a comprehensive understanding of the molecular architecture of the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed. As illustrated in Fig.S5, all samples manifest characteristic peaks indicative of CN polymers. These include the bending vibration of heptazine units (C-N-C) at 807.9 cm−1 and the stretching vibrations of aromatic CN heterocycles spanning the range of 1750–1200 cm−1 [27]. Additionally, a newly identified peak at 2180 cm−1, attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of cyanide (C≡N), exhibits increased intensity following molten salt treatment [28]. Moreover, the peaks located at 995 and 3510 cm−1 correspond to O-H groups present on the HC-C3N5 surface, highlighting the crucial role of edge cyanide and polar hydroxyl groups in enhancing O2 adsorption and activation. This ultimately improves the selectivity of g-C3N5 towards O2 and facilitates the in situ generation of H2O2 during the photocatalytic process [29].

In pursuit of a comprehensive understanding of the molecular arrangement and atomic milieu within Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, the specimens underwent thorough scrutiny employing X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and meticulous elemental analysis. XPS survey spectra in Fig. 2c unveil a predominant presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) across all samples. Notably, the HC-C3N5 variant exhibits an additional potassium (K) element doping, whereas the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 variant manifests an augmented iron (Fe) content, thus affirming the successful incorporation of Fe atoms requisite for catalytic Fenton reactions, which corresponding to the iron observed in the XPS survey spectrum of the Fe-CDs (Fig.S6a). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of Fe-CDs can be deconvolved into three peaks of 283.9, 284.6 and 285.2, belonging to the C-C, C-O and C = O bands, respectively (Fig.S6b). In addition, C1s XPS spectra of C3N5-based samples (Fig. 2d) discernible peaks at 288, 287.6, 285.7, and 284.3 eV correspond to triazole motifs adorning the CN framework [30], carbon atoms bonded triply to adjacent nitrogen atoms, specifically N = C-N within the nitrogen-aromatic ring, C≡N arising from terminal amino or C-NHX conversions, and C-C/C = C within physically adsorbed carbonaceous entities [31]. Particularly noteworthy is the substantially elevated on the peak at 285.7 eV of HC-C3N5 and Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 relative to B-C3N5, primarily attributable to comparable binding energies of C≡N and NHX groups, with C-NHX groups emerging via dehydrogenation of C-NHX moieties, thereby further substantiating the presence of the −C≡N moiety [32]. In the N 1 s spectrum of Fe-CDs (Fig.S6c), there are three binding energies at 398.1 eV, 398.6 eV, and 399.2 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. Concurrently, the N 1 s XPS spectrum of HC-C3N5 (Fig. 2e) can be resolved into peaks at 397.6, 398.4, 400.2, and 404.1 eV, attributed respectively to the C-N = C, N-(C)3, −NHx and π-π* excitation state [33]. It is noteworthy that in the N 1 s XPS spectrum of 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, in addition to the four peaks corresponding to HC-C3N5, a new signal peak at 399.5 eV is observed, corresponding to the Fe-N bond, which can not only facilitate more efficacious charge separation and transfer but also expedites the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycles, thus catalyzing OH generation during the Fenton process [34]. Additionally, the observed shift in the N 1 s peak position in the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite, compared to HC-C3N5, can be attributed to the incorporation of the Fe element. As illustrated in Fig.S6d, the high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum of the Fe-CDs demonstrates that the Fe 2p3/2 component can be meticulously deconvoluted into two distinct peaks at 708.0 eV and 709.9 eV, which are attributable to Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively. The XPS spectra were also collected for Fe2O3 and chloro (protoporphyrinato) iron (III) (C34H36O4N4Fe), both of which contain Fe-N4 structures (Fig. 2f). The Fe 2p XPS spectrum of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 closely mirrors that of Hemin but is notably lower than that of iron oxide. This discrepancy is due to the reduced Fe binding energy resulting from nitrogen coordination, providing robust evidence for the presence of Fe-N active centers in the catalyst [35]. Furthermore, the Fe 2p3/2 peak in Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 splits into 710.8 and 710.0 eV, corresponding to Fe3+ and Fe2+, respectively. This splitting indicates the enhanced reactivity of the Fenton reaction, facilitated by the Fe3+↔Fe2+ redox cycle as an active center [36]. In addition, following the anchoring of Fe-CDs, an additional peak at 603 cm−1 appears in the Raman spectrum of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 spectrum, attributed to the in-plane Fe-N stretching vibration. This peak predominantly results from the displacement of nitrogen atoms surrounding the Fe center, providing further evidence that Fe-CDs are embedded into the highly crystalline HC-C3N5 nanosheets via Fe-N chemical bonds (Fig.S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ generation and decomposition of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-self-Fenton reaction system is characterized by an advanced oxidative process whereby hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is generated in situ during photocatalysis, subsequently undergoing reaction with Fe2+ ions to produce radicals [37]. This study undertakes a comprehensive investigation of the photo-self-Fenton system based on Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, wherein the performances in-situ H2O2 generation and antibiotic degradation are evaluated. Within the constructed photo-self-Fenton system of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, HC-C3N5 serves as a precursor for in situ H2O2 generation, while Fe-CDs serve as the source of Fe2+ ions in the Fenton reaction. Experiment results indicate that Fe-CDs samples exhibit minimal generation of H2O2, whereas CDs/HC-C3N5 demonstrate significantly enhanced photocatalytic capability for H2O2 generation compared to pristine HC-C3N5 after the incorporation of CDs, which convincingly confirms that the introduction of CDs as additional active sites enhances the in-situ generation capacity of the catalyst for H2O2 (Fig. 3 a). Notably, the H2O2 generation of 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite material is slightly lower than that of 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5, with the fitted rate constant (k) for the first 60 min decreasing from 2.94 to 2.39 (Fig. 3b and c), potentially due to the activation reaction between Fe2+ in the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 and the in-situ generated H2O2 [38]. It is noteworthy that beyond 60 min, the production rate of all samples exhibited a marked decline, which can be attributed to the progressive depletion of oxygen concentration over the course of the reaction, which consequently results in a diminished reaction rate. Activated H2O2 generates OH radicals, which subsequently react with a portion of H2O2, thereby decelerating H2O2 production (e- + Fe3+ → Fe2+, Fe2+ + H2O2 → OH + OH– + Fe3+) [39]. To further elucidate the reaction mechanism underlying photocatalytic H2O2 production, adjustments were made to the reaction conditions (Fig. 3d). It was found that H2O2 detection was nearly impossible in the absence of visible light or photocatalysts, confirming the decisive role of photocatalysts and visible light irradiation in H2O2 production [40]. Moreover, the H2O2 yield was significantly diminished in an argon atmosphere, whereas a marked increase was observed in an oxygen atmosphere. This observation suggests that H2O2 generation by Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 is facilitated through the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) involving dissolved oxygen. To further elucidate the mechanism of H2O2 production, capture experiments were conducted using p-benzoquinone and silver nitrate as scavengers for superoxide radicals (O2 –) and electrons (e-). Upon the removal of the influences of O2 – and e-, a significant reduction in the H2O2 yield of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 was observed, indicating that the ORR on Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 is primarily mediated through a consecutive two-step single-electron indirect reduction pathway (e- + O2 → O2 –, O2 – + 2H + e- → H2O2)[41]. During the process of in-situ H2O2 generation for photo-self-Fenton degradation, the simultaneous occurrence of H2O2 generation and decomposition is observed, with the generated H2O2 subsequently reacting with Fe2+ to generate OH radicals that can catalyze the degradation of pollutants [42]. Consequently, in-situ H2O2 decomposition tests were conducted, as portrayed in Fig. 3e-f and Fig.S8, revealing that within a 120-min timeframe, the H2O2 decomposition rates for Fe-CDs, HC-C3N5, 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5 and 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 were found to be 50 %, 33 %, 27 %, and 38 %, respectively. The H2O2 decomposition rate of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 was observed to be higher than that of pure HC-C3N5 and CDs/HC-C3N5 but lower than that of pure Fe-CDs, indicating that Fe-CDs in Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 can be used as the active site for in-situ H2O2 activation, thereby enhancing the overall photocatalytic degradation activity. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 3g, the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite exhibited remarkable stability in its photocatalytic performance for H2O2 production across four successive cycles. The XRD patterns of the composite catalyst, both pre- and post-reaction, displayed no discernible changes (Fig. 3h). In addition, post-reaction TEM images (Fig. 3i) confirmed that the Fe-CDs remained securely embedded within the HC-C3N5 matrix, thereby corroborating the structural stability of the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite.

A photo-self-Fenton system between Fe-CDs and HC-C3N5 was successfully established, in which photo-induced H2O2 generation is mediated by HC-C3N5, while the decomposition of H2O2 leading to the generation of an active intermediate is based on Fe-CDs. The evaluation of photo-self-Fenton degradation performance of the prepared samples, utilizing 50 mg/L tetracycline (TC) as the target pollutant, was depicted in Fig. 4 a. Equilibrium in adsorption–desorption was reached within 0.5 h preceding the photocatalytic reaction. Subsequently, under visible light irradiation, a degradation rate of 11.1 % towards TC was exhibited by Fe-CDs, whereas HC-C3N5 demonstrated a degradation rate of 42.7 % towards TC after 120 min of irradiation. Compared to the individual components (Fe-CDs and HC-C3N5), the prepared Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite materials (i.e., 1 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, 2 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 and 4 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5) showcased enhanced activity. The introduction of Fe-CDs significantly enhanced the degradation efficiency of TC. This facilitated an improvement in the composite system's charge separation efficiency and also enabled the photo-self-Fenton reaction. Additionally, the pseudo-first-order model successfully described the TC degradation process, and the corresponding reaction rate constants (k) were calculated, as shown in Fig. 4b and c. Notably, the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite exhibited the highest k value, being 37.3 times and 3.3 times greater than that of Fe-CDs and HC-C3N5, respectively. Furthermore, the mineralization degree of TC in the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 system was evaluated by measuring the total organic carbon (TOC). As depicted in Fig.S9, the system achieved a mineralization degree of 47.9 % within 1 h, indicating that the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 constructed in this study operates with high efficiency and stability. Overall, the concentration of Fe2+ ions played a crucial role as a reagent in influencing the degradation efficiency of organic pollutants during the Fenton process. [43]. The degradation rate of TC improved as the concentration of Fe-CDs increased from 1 % to 3 %. However, further elevating the Fe2+ concentration caused a reduction in the degradation efficiency. This decline is attributed to the excess Fe-CDs being oxidized by OH radicals, leading to the formation of Fe3+ and HO– [44]. Hence, 3 % was selected as the optimal Fe-CDs concentration in Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite for subsequent investigations.

Moreover, as depicted in Fig.S10, under the same irradiation time, the degradation efficiency of the mechanically mixed composition of Fe-CDs and HC-C3N5 (3 % Fe-CDs and HC-C3N5 mixture) was significantly lower compared to 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, reaffirming the advantage of Fe-CDs embedded within HC-C3N5 as active sites, thereby accelerating the catalytic cycle between Fe2+ and Fe3+ and generating more OH [45]. This finding suggests that the photo-Fenton reaction involves more than just the sum of its individual reactions. To verify the role of H2O2 produced by the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite, a radical capture experiment was carried out based on the methodology outlined in previous studies [46]. The presence of catalase resulted in a significant reduction in TC degradation efficiency, from 78.5 % to 24.1 % within 120 min (Fig.S11). This substantial decrease further confirms that H2O2 plays a crucial role in the photo-self-Fenton system of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 for TC degradation. The experimental results illustrated in Fig. 4d and e were meticulously analyzed to evaluate the degradation efficiency of three different mechanisms: Fenton, photo-Fenton, and photo-self-Fenton, in a solution containing 50 mg/L of TC. It was unveiled that the photo-Fenton mechanism predominantly dictates the degradation process, markedly surpassing its counterparts. Specifically, the degradation efficiency of 50 mg/L TC using the Fenton system composed of Fe-CDs and H2O2 was observed to be 14.6 %. However, when subjected to visible light irradiation, the photo-Fenton system exhibited a significantly higher degradation efficiency of 65.3 %. Remarkably, the photo-self-Fenton system, utilizing 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 under visible light excitation, outperformed both the Fenton and photo-Fenton systems, achieving an impressive degradation efficiency of 78.4 % for TC (50 mg/L). Furthermore, the apparent rate constants (k) derived from fitting the pseudo-first-order model indicated that the photo-self-Fenton system's k value was 15.71 times and 2.61 times greater than those of the Fenton and photo-Fenton systems, respectively (Fig.S12). This substantial increase in rate constants underscores the superior efficacy of the photo-self-Fenton system in antibiotic degradation. As illustrated in Fig.S13, the oxidation states of iron in 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 during the photo-self-Fenton reaction were meticulously evaluated. It was observed that significant alterations in the iron oxidation states within the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 matrix ensued following the photo-self-Fenton reaction. A marked increase in the relative content of Fe3+ was detected, while a significant decrease in the relative content of Fe2+ was noted. This indicates that the Fenton reaction (Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + OH– + OH) was indeed initiated during the photo-self-Fenton process, resulting in the production of a substantial amount of highly oxidative OH radicals.

To further elucidate the role of Fe-CDs as active sites for the in-situ activation of H2O2 in the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite, comparative studies were conducted using CDs/HC-C3N5, iron porphyrin, and Fe2O3 as alternative iron sources. Each was combined with HC-C3N5 similarly, and their photo-self-Fenton degradation efficiencies for TC were subsequently evaluated (Fig. 4f). The results revealed that the photocatalytic performance of CDs/HC-C3N5 was approximately half that of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, indicating that the mere incorporation of CDs into HC-C3N5, without the presence of iron, was inadequate for facilitating the activation of H2O2 into OH radicals, thus resulting in lower photocatalytic degradation efficiency. Moreover, the addition of iron porphyrins and Fe2O3 on HC-C3N5 did not effectively enhance the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TC, suggesting that the Fe-O configuration provided by these iron oxide nanoparticles is ineffective in activating H2O2 [21]. It was only the incorporation of Fe-CDs that successfully introduced active sites within the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite, thereby increasing H2O2 yield and accelerating the activation process of H2O2 through the catalytic cycling of Fe2+/Fe3+ in Fe-CDs, ultimately improving the photo-Fenton degradation performance. Additionally, the introduction of weakly acidic anions promotes the conversion of iron ions into less soluble iron hydroxides, which hampers the activation of H2O2 and the degradation of pollutants. The incorporation of certain inorganic anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, and CO3 2–) into the system simulates conditions found in real aquatic environments (Fig. 4g). The results indicate that the addition of SO4 2- has minimal impact on degradation efficiency, while the introduction of CO3 2– and Cl- reduces degradation rates to 53.6 % and 56.7 %, respectively. This reduction is likely due to the rapid deactivation of reactive oxygen species and the disruption of active Fe2+ sites, highlighting that the depletion of iron ions and reactive oxygen species in the photo-Fenton system can hinder the decomposition of H2O2 [47].

Besides, the stability of photocatalytic materials is crucial for the practical application of the photo-Fenton system [18]. Therefore, the initial pH of the TC solution was adjusted using 0.1 M hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide solutions (Fig. 4h). Lowering the pH from 6 to 3 increased the degradation efficiency from 70.1 % to 85.9 %, attributed to the accelerated catalytic cycle between Fe3+ and Fe2+ in acidic conditions, which catalyzes the decomposition of H2O2 and generates more OH radicals to enhance TC degradation. Surprisingly, increasing the pH to 8 and 9 also resulted in a significant increase in TC degradation efficiency with 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5. Typically, the Fenton process is hindered in alkaline solutions due to the formation of Fe-based hydroxides from Fe ions reacting with OH–, which inhibits the catalytic reaction between Fe ions and H2O2 [48]. However, under alkaline conditions, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 system may be due to the stability of Fe-N bonds in Fe-CDs, which prevent the formation of Fe-based hydroxide precipitates. Additionally, Fe-CDs serve as active sites for photocatalytic reactions, promoting the generation of more electron-hole pairs and enhancing degradation efficiency. Therefore, the increased degradation efficiency under acidic conditions primarily stems from the photo-Fenton process, while under alkaline conditions, it results from both the incorporation of Fe-CDs as active sites and the generation of more photocarriers by Fe-CDs. To further confirm the broad applicability of the photo-Fenton degradation process mediated by 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, a series of pollutant degradation experiments were conducted. The results clearly demonstrated the remarkable photo-Fenton degradation efficacy of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 for various broad-spectrum antibiotics, consistent with expectations (Fig.S14). After six cycles, the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 maintained substantial catalytic activity in degrading TC (Fig. 4i), In contrast, a significant decline in performance after six cycles was exhibited by the photocatalytic materials composed of HC-C3N5 and iron porphyrin or Fe2O3 (Fig.S15). This phenomenon suggests that the anchoring of Fe-CDs in the form of carbon dots on HC-C3N5 effectively prevents the aggregation of iron particles, a prevalent issue in iron oxides, thereby enhancing the material's stability. Additionally, analysis via inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) (Table S1 and Fig.S16) revealed that only a minimal amount of iron ions was detected in the solution post-degradation of TC by 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, whereas a substantial amount of iron ions was detected during the cyclic tests of the photocatalytic materials composed of HC-C3N5 with iron porphyrin and Fe2O3. This finding further indicates that the nanoscale dimensions of Fe-CDs and the stable Fe-N bonds ensure the firm anchoring of iron species, preventing their detachment, thereby imparting significant stability to the photocatalyst during the photo-Fenton process.

In order to deeply study the efficient photo-self-Fenton activity of Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 in TC degradation, the band structure of the composite was assessed through the utilization of UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) and valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) [49]. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, all samples exhibited a characteristic absorption peak around 380 nm, attributed to the π-π* electronic transitions of heptazine units. The integration of CDs and Fe-doped CDs induced a slight redshift in the absorption edge of HC-C3N5, likely due to the introduction of mid-gap states formed by the CDs. The optical bandgaps of the samples were deduced from the Tauc plots, correlating (αhν)r with photon energy (hν). As shown in Fig. 5b, the incorporation of CDs and Fe-CDs reduced the bandgap of HC-C3N5 from 2.74 to 2.71 and 2.69 eV, respectively. Additionally, VB edge positions determined based on VB-XPS analysis revealed E v values of 1.51, 1.41, and 1.37 eV for HC-C3N5, 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5, and 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, respectively (Fig. 5c). Correspondingly, the conduction band edge (E c) positions were identified as −1.23, −1.30 and −1.32 eV, respectively (Fig. 5d). The minor shifts in the band structure upon the introduction of CDs and Fe-CDs suggest that the enhanced in-situ generation of H2O2 in Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 are not solely due to the deepening of color and broadening of the absorption spectrum through CDs-based materials integration. Instead, these improvements are derived from the dual-functional active sites of Fe-CDs, which facilitate charge separation and transfer, minimize recombination, and thus modulate the dynamics of interfacial charge transport. Meanwhile, Fe-CDs could provide the Fe source for the photo-self-Fenton process and accelerates the activation process of H2O2. Using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a radical scavenger, experiments employing electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy to capture superoxide (O2 –) and hydroxyl (OH) radicals provided further evidence for the in-situ generation and activation of H2O2 catalytic sites by Fe-CDs in Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5. As given in Fig. 5e, under illumination, signals from DMPO-O2 – were detected in HC-C3N5, 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5, and 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 samples, moreover, compared to HC-C3N5, suspensions of 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5 and 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 exhibited more pronounced ESR signal enhancements under light, indicating that CDs as active sites for the in-situ generation of H2O2 facilitate the formation of additional O2 –, thus benefiting the rapid production of H2O2. Notably, the O2 – signals from 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 showed no significant change compared to 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5, suggesting that the incorporation of iron has not been adversely affected the performance of in-situ H2O2 generation. Additionally, under illumination conditions, DMPO-OH signals were detected in HC-C3N5, 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5, and 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 (Fig. 5f). The results indicate that the suspension of 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 exhibited more pronounced signal enhancement compared to HC-C3N5 and 3 % CDs/HC-C3N5, demonstrating that Fe-CDs not only serve as sites for H2O2 generation but also act as activation sites for H2O2 based on the Fe2+/Fe3+ catalytic cycles, generating more OH radicals, thus enhancing the photo-self-Fenton degradation reaction for antibiotic degradation. To substantiate the role of Fe-CDs in the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite as dual active sites enhancing exciton dissociation and photocatalytic efficiency, the effect of Fe doping on the photoluminescence (PL) emission intensity was scrutinized. Previous research has shown that C3N5 exhibits a prominent PL peak at approximately 468 nm, attributable to the direct recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes [50]. Compared to HC-C3N5, the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composites exhibited a slight redshift and a reduction in PL intensity by about 30 %, indicating that the incorporation of Fe-CDs promotes electron-hole separation (Fig. 5g). As the Fe-CDs content increased, a progressive decline in the emission peak intensity was observed, signifying that Fe-CDs serve as dual active sites for H2O2 generation and activation, thereby enhancing the separation of photogenerated carriers. Time-resolved PL spectroscopy was employed to further investigate the dynamics of photogenerated carriers, with a multi-exponential fitting analysis providing insights into the average PL lifetime and its components, as detailed in Table S2. The fitted fluorescence decay included τ1 and τ2, where τ1 corresponds to the fluorescence decay time from the semiconductor conduction band excited electrons to the valence band, and τ2 represents the recombination fluorescence decay time of surface photoinduced electron-hole pairs [51]. The average PL lifetimes for HC-C3N5 and 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 were determined to be 3.9 and 2.4 ns, respectively (Fig.S17), with the shorter lifetime indicative of more efficient separation of photoexcited electrons and holes. Moreover, the presence of Fe-CDs was found to reduce charge transfer resistance. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements demonstrated that the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite displayed a smaller semicircle in the Nyquist plot compared to HC-C3N5 (Fig.S18), suggesting that Fe-CDs as dual active sites in the composite enhance the conductivity at the HC-C3N5/electrolyte interface, thereby facilitating more efficient electron transfer.

Based on the detailed analysis conducted in this study, a comprehensive photo-self-Fenton mechanism for the degradation of TC by the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite has been proposed, as depicted in Fig. 6 . The incorporation of Fe-CDs as in situ active sites for H2O2 formation significantly enhances the separation and transfer of photo-induced charges within HC-C3N5. This process mitigates charge recombination, thereby optimizing interfacial charge transport dynamics and improving the efficiency of H2O2 production. Simultaneously, the ferrous ions provided by Fe-CDs act as highly efficient Fenton reagents, catalyzing the in-situ activation of H2O2 generated by the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite. This results in the establishment of a self-sustaining photo-self-Fenton system. Under visible light irradiation, HC-C3N5 efficiently generates separated electrons and holes (Eq. (1)). The electrons are then transferred to Fe-CDs, which act as active sites for a two-step, single-electron indirect reduction pathway, reducing O2 to H2O2 (Eqs. (2) and (3)). Subsequently, Fe2+ within Fe-CDs reacts with H2O2 to produce highly reactive OH radicals and HO– (Eq. (4)), while the photo-generated electrons assist in converting Fe3+ back to Fe2+ (Eq. (5)). This cyclic conversion between Fe3+ and Fe2+ significantly enhances the photo-self-Fenton reaction efficiency for degrading TC pollutants. Furthermore, Fe-CDs, as in situ generation and activation sites for H2O2, effectively increase the utilization rate of H2O2, thereby facilitating the efficient production of OH radicals. Ultimately, TC can be degraded into non-toxic and harmless small molecules (CO2 and H2O) by the as-produced ROS radicals (Eq. (6)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of degradation products and degradation pathway
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the photo-self-Fenton degradation pathways of TC using Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) was utilized to identify the principal intermediates during the reaction. Upon extending the irradiation time to 100 min, a marked decrease in the signal intensity at m/z = 445, corresponding to deprotonated TC, was observed [18]. This reduction coincided with the emergence of new mass signal indicative of intermediate by-products formed during TC degradation. Analysis of the mass spectra identified 16 major intermediates, labeled P1-P16, with their detailed information presented in Fig.S19. Based on the molecular formulas of these intermediates, three primary degradation pathways for the photo-self-Fenton process of TC by Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 were proposed (Fig. 7 ). In Pathway 1, in the absence of light, TC is initially attacked by OH and O2–, resulting in the sequential loss of methyl, hydroxyl, amino, and aldehyde groups, forming intermediate P1 (m/z = 329). Further ring opening, hydrogenation, and oxidation processes lead to the formation of intermediates P2 (m/z = 267), P3 (m/z = 242), P4 (m/z = 235), and P5 (m/z = 195). Under light irradiation, TC molecules undergo n-demethylation and hydroxylation due to the action of photogenerated OH and O2 –. Subsequent deamination, dehydration, and cyclization processes yield low molecular weight intermediates P6-P8 [52]. Pathway 2 involves the hydroxylation of TC by OH and O2 –, producing P9 (m/z = 461), which subsequently undergoes dehydration to form P10 (m/z = 364). Further attack on P10 results in ring opening, generating P11 (m/z = 338) [53]. Finally, P11 is then degraded through continuous ring-opening and loss of functional groups, producing smaller organic molecules, identified as P12 (m/z = 60). In Pathway 3, TC undergoes demethylation at the C position, leading to the removal of –CH3, –OH, –CONH2, and –OH groups, and demethylation at the N position, resulting in the removal of –CH3, forming intermediate P13 (m/z = 329) [54]. The subsequent degradation of P13 produces P14 (m/z = 178), which further degrades to yield P15 (m/z = 115). Under free radical oxidation, these intermediates are ultimately converted into P16 (m/z = 60). Finally, TC and these intermediates undergo further decomposition into CO2 and H2O, etc.

Using the quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) method, the ECOSAR program was employed to predict the acute and chronic toxicities of TC and its degradation products towards fish, water fleas, and algae. The results, as presented in Table S3 and Fig. 8 a, indicate a marked increase in acute toxicity to fish and algae during the degradation of TC into P3, P9, P13, and P14. This increase in toxicity is likely attributed to the heightened metabolic reactivity stemming from C-C unsaturation in allyl and vinyl structures, thereby enhancing aquatic toxicity [18]. However, as TC further degrades into P4, P5, P7, P8, P12, P15, and P16, the concentration of the toxic moieties diminishes, leading to reduced acute toxicity compared to the parent compound. Fig. 8b illustrates that the chronic toxicity of TC degradation products gradually decreases during the photo-self-Fenton degradation process. This reduction is possibly due to the decrease in toxic moieties such as acrylamide. Ultimately, TC degrades into harmless products for aquatic organisms, including P12, P16, CO2, and H2O, through photo-self-Fenton degradation over the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite. These findings underscore the significant potential of the Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 photo-self-Fenton system for effective TC degradation.photo-..

In conclusion, this study successfully synthesized Fe-CDs embedded within HC-C3N5 nanosheets, serving as dual-active sites for the treatment of antibiotic wastewater through the photo-self-Fenton degradation reaction. By optimizing the mass ratio between Fe-CDs and HC-C3N5, the 3 % Fe-CDs/HC-C3N5 composite demonstrated exceptional performance in the photo-self-Fenton degradation of antibiotic pollutants, achieving a degradation efficiency of approximately 74.1 % for 50 mg/L TC after 120 min of irradiation. The enhanced degradation efficiency can be attributed to two key factors: (i) Fe-CDs act as active sites that facilitate superior light-induced charge separation and transfer, leading to the in-situ generation of H2O2 on HC-C3N5; and (ii) Fe-CDs provide solid-phase Fe species that maximize the activation of in-situ generated H2O2 to produce highly oxidative OH and O2 – species for the photo-self-Fenton reaction. This study offers a novel design strategy for developing efficient photo-self-Fenton systems based on Fe-CDs for antibiotic degradation, establishing a robust foundation for advancing more efficient and cost-effective technologies to address antibiotic pollution.

Yu Shen: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuhan Wang: Data curation. Pengnian Shan: Data curation. Rui Xu: Data curation. Xinhai Sun: Data curation. Jianhua Hou: Data curation. Feng Guo: Data curation. Chunsheng Li: Resources. Weilong Shi: Data curation.
